title: Mediterranean diet & cardiovascular risk (4 turns · retraction twist + convergent evidence)
note: Real, checkable. Exercises: a world-update/retraction flavor (PREDIMED retracted then republished — preview of phase-2 D operation), a methodological contradiction, scope refinement, and convergent multi-source support with a co-cited source.

trace.content: Does the Mediterranean diet reduce cardiovascular events?

arguments.q: PREDIMED Mediterranean diet cardiovascular events randomized trial NEJM

trace.content: PREDIMED (Estruch et al., NEJM 2013) was a large multicenter RCT in Spain reporting that a Mediterranean diet supplemented with extra-virgin olive oil or nuts reduced major cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, cardiovascular death) versus a control low-fat diet, hazard ratio ~0.70.
The 2013 PREDIMED RCT reported the Mediterranean diet cut major cardiovascular events by roughly 30% versus a low-fat control.
Wasn't PREDIMED retracted because of a randomization problem?

arguments.q: PREDIMED 2018 retraction republication randomization protocol deviations

trace.content: In 2018 the original 2013 PREDIMED paper was RETRACTED and REPUBLISHED after irregularities in randomization were found (some participants were assigned by clinic/household rather than individually). The re-analysis excluding/ reweighting those participants still found a similar reduction in cardiovascular events.
The original 2013 randomization was flawed, which undermines the original report as published; this is a methodological strike against taking the 2013 paper at face value.
However, the 2018 republished re-analysis corrected for the randomization issue and still showed a comparable benefit, so the conclusion survives in a refined, methodologically-sounder form.
Is there independent evidence beyond PREDIMED?

arguments.q: Lyon Diet Heart Study Mediterranean diet secondary prevention cohort evidence

trace.content: The Lyon Diet Heart Study (de Lorgeril et al.) — a secondary-prevention RCT in post-MI patients — found a Mediterranean-style diet markedly reduced recurrent cardiac events, and multiple prospective cohort studies report lower cardiovascular mortality with higher Mediterranean-diet adherence.
Independent trial and cohort evidence converges with the corrected PREDIMED result, so the benefit does not rest on the flawed 2013 analysis alone.

In 2018 the original 2013 PREDIMED paper was RETRACTED and REPUBLISHED after irregularities in randomization were found (some participants were assigned by clinic/household rather than individually). The re-analysis excluding/ reweighting those participants still found a similar reduction in cardiovascular events.

Verdict: the Mediterranean diet reduces cardiovascular events — supported by the republished PREDIMED re-analysis, the Lyon Diet Heart Study, and convergent cohort data — with the caveat that the original 2013 PREDIMED randomization was flawed and should not be cited as-is.
